1.synthesis: James diagnoses the community's internal conflicts as rooted in disordered desires: "What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this — that your passions are at war within you?" (Jas 4:1). You desire and do not have, so you fight; you do not have because you do not ask; you ask and do not receive because you ask with wrong motives — to spend on your own pleasures (Jas 4:2–3). Adulterers! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? God gives more grace — "God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble" (Prov 3:34; Jas 4:4–6). Submit to God; resist the devil and he will flee; draw near to God and he will draw near to you — cleanse your hands and purify your hearts, you double-minded (Jas 4:7–8). Be wretched, mourn, and weep; humble yourselves before the Lord and he will exalt you (Jas 4:9–10). Do not speak evil against one another — there is one Lawgiver and Judge; who are you to judge your neighbor? (Jas 4:11–12).
1.historical_context: The military vocabulary James applies to internal community disputes — polemos (wars) and machē (battles) — was striking applied to congregational conflict. The diagnosis of their origin in hēdonai (pleasures, passions) "warring in your members" (v.1) echoes Plato's account in the Republic (Book 4) of the soul's internal warfare between its rational and appetitive parts, and the Stoic analysis of pathē (passions) as the source of all social disorder. The prophetic address "adulterers!" (v.4) — applying the image of Israel as YHWH's unfaithful spouse (Hos 2:2–13; Jer 3:1–10) — frames the community's worldliness as covenant infidelity rather than mere moral failure. The citation of Proverbs 3:34 LXX (v.6: "God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble") is shared with 1 Pet 5:5 and is one of the earliest NT uses of a shared scriptural catechesis about divine reversal. The "draw near to God and he will draw near to you" of v.8 echoes Zech 1:3 and Mal 3:7 — the prophetic call to return that characterizes James's entire pastoral posture.
1.literary_structure: The section is built around a series of inversions that structure the path from conflict to restoration: desires → conflict (v.1); asking → not receiving (v.3); world-friendship → God-enmity (v.4); pride → resistance from God / humility → grace from God (v.6); resist devil → he flees / draw near to God → he draws near (vv.7–8); mourning → exaltation (vv.9–10). Each inversion exposes the community's false assumptions: that conflict is caused by external circumstances rather than internal desires; that getting what you want constitutes receiving; that social success measures God's favor. The section closes by extending the anti-judgment principle to speech about neighbors (vv.11–12) — the one who slanders a neighbor has presumed on the divine prerogative of judgment, the same transgression that structures the entire passage.
1.theological_application: The diagnosis that community conflict has its root in unfulfilled desire ("you desire and do not have," v.2) is a pastoral anthropology: the community's external fractures have internal sources. The answer James proposes is not conflict-management technique but reorientation of desire — from self-gratification (asking to spend on pleasures, v.3) to God-aligned asking. The double-minded person (v.8: "purify your hearts, you double-minded") has divided loyalty between God and the world: the mourning James calls for (v.9) is the grief of repentance over that divided loyalty, not a performance of asceticism. The humble person who is exalted (v.10) mirrors the pattern of Mary's Magnificat: God scatters the proud and lifts up the humble. Humility before God is the precondition for genuine community — those who have stopped competing with God can stop competing with each other.
1.christology: The Jesus tradition underlies James 4 at multiple points without being explicitly cited. "Resist the devil and he will flee" (v.7) resonates with Jesus's wilderness temptation, where the devil departed after Jesus's sustained resistance (Matt 4:11). The teaching "do not speak evil against one another; there is one Lawgiver and Judge" (vv.11–12) reflects the Sermon on the Mount's extension of the sixth commandment to contemptuous speech (Matt 5:21–22). The divine reversal — "humble yourselves before the Lord and he will exalt you" (v.10) — is the beatitude of the meek (Matt 5:5) and the self-humbling of the Son who was exalted after his kenotic descent (Phil 2:8–9). James consistently presents community ethics as the outworking of the same divine pattern that the gospel announces: the way down is the way up, and the cross-shaped pattern of humility-to-exaltation governs both Christ's path and his community's.

13.synthesis: The letter addresses those who plan: "Today or tomorrow we will go to this or that city, spend a year there, carry on business and make money" (Jas 4:13). They do not know what tomorrow will bring — they are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes (v.14). Instead they ought to say, "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" (v.15). As it is, they boast in their arrogance; all such boasting is evil (v.16). To the one who knows what is right and does not do it — that is sin (Jas 4:17).
13.historical_context: The merchants who plan commercial travel (v.13) represent a specific sociological group within or adjacent to James's communities: Jewish merchants engaged in the diaspora trade networks that connected cities across the Roman Empire. The Epistle's diaspora address (1:1: "to the twelve tribes scattered abroad") situates its readers in exactly this mobile commercial world. The brevity-of-life image ("a mist that appears for a little while," v.14) uses atmis (mist, vapor, steam) which directly echoes Ecclesiastes' hebel (vapor, breath, vanity) — the word used 38 times in Qoheleth as the refrain of existence's transience. The conditional formula "if the Lord wills" (ean ho Kyrios thelēsē, v.15) was the standard pious acknowledgment of divine sovereignty in both Jewish (im yirtzeh ha-Shem) and Greek (ean ho theos thelēi) culture — James is not introducing a novel practice but insisting that the community's commercial planning actually embody the divine-sovereignty acknowledgment that their vocabulary already assumes. The alazoneia (arrogance/boasting) of v.16 was the vice of the alazōn — the braggart who claims more than they possess — a stock figure in ancient ethics.
13.literary_structure: The section shifts from communal conflict (vv.1–12) to individual presumption, but the underlying concern is the same: the displacement of God from the center of practical life. The merchant who plans without acknowledging divine sovereignty is not guilty of malice but of practical atheism — living as if the future were theirs to command. The corrective is not the elimination of planning but the integration of divine sovereignty into the planning: "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" (v.15). The closing aphorism (v.17: "to the one who knows the right thing and does not do it, it is sin") expands the scope from commercial planning to all areas of life: sin is not only the commission of wrong but the omission of known right. This is the wisdom tradition's foundational insight about the nature of knowledge-accountability: knowing creates obligation.
13.theological_application: The "mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes" (v.14) is not merely a rhetorical flourish but a theological claim about human temporality: the creature who lives as if their future were their own to dispose has misunderstood their position before the Creator. The correct posture — "If the Lord wills" (v.15) — is not fatalism or passivity but the integration of genuine trust into practical planning: make plans, yes, but hold them loosely, knowing that the one who sustains your life (v.14: "if the Lord wills, we will live") is sovereign over both the planning and the execution. The final verse (v.17) extends the ethics of omission: the community member who knows the right thing and does not do it shares the guilt of the merchant who knows they are a mist and lives as if they are eternal. Knowledge of the good creates accountability for the good.
13.christology: The epistle's christology appears here obliquely: the Lord whose will governs life and commerce is the Lord Jesus, as James's address ("slave of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ," 1:1) establishes. The merchant who should say "if the Lord wills" acknowledges that Jesus is Lord of the commercial world, not merely of the religious sphere. This is the lordship claim that the early church confessed in its counter-cultural fullness: "Jesus is Lord" means that the planning of trade routes, the calculation of profit, and the projection of commercial calendars are all subject to his sovereignty. The practical integration of divine-will acknowledgment into business planning is a small but concrete expression of the comprehensive lordship that James — like Paul — insists cannot be sectioned off into a "religious" domain while the rest of life proceeds autonomously.
